texts.label: text
texts.orig: Despite the recent change in the behavior of the energy sector towards renewable matrices, it is expected that fossil fuels continue to be the  main  source  of  energy  for  the  next  years.  Natural  gas  (NG),  for example, is considered a primary energy source with a high H/C ratio and low emission of pollutants in comparison to oil and coal, so it may smooth this transition  of  energy  matrices  (Kan  et  al.,  2019;  Barbosa et al., 2019; Chu et al., 2019).
texts.text: Despite the recent change in the behavior of the energy sector towards renewable matrices, it is expected that fossil fuels continue to be the  main  source  of  energy  for  the  next  years.  Natural  gas  (NG),  for example, is considered a primary energy source with a high H/C ratio and low emission of pollutants in comparison to oil and coal, so it may smooth this transition  of  energy  matrices  (Kan  et  al.,  2019;  Barbosa et al., 2019; Chu et al., 2019).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Natural gas contains, in addition to the main component methane (CH4), other minor impurities such as carbon dioxide (CO2), hydrogen sulfide  (H2S),  nitrogen  (N2),  water  (H2O),  ethane  (C2H6),  propane (C3H8) and toluene (C7H8) (Arinelli et al., 2019; Oliveira et al., 2019; Okazaki et al., 2019). Both CO2 and H2S in contact with moisture react and produce hydrates responsible for corroding equipment and piping. Thus, the CO2 concentration must be reduced to 2 -3% mol, respecting Brazilian  and  American  regulations,  for  example  (Reis  et  al.,  2017; Araújo  et  al.,  2017).  Therefore,  the  removal  of  these  acid  gases  is essential to guarantee a process with less maintenance frequency and obtain a product with more affordable value at the end of the production chain. Moreover, the removed CO2 can be used in the enhanced oil recovery (EOR) (Arinelli et al., 2019; Oliveira et al., 2019; Okazaki et al., 2019).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This review discusses recent advances in the development of materials and membranes with the potential for use in hostile environments and their efficiency in separating CO2  and CH4. Depending on the gas treatment operating condition, the membranes are exposed to harsh conditions (pressures above 40 bar), which is a challenge to ensure performance. Polymeric, inorganic, and mixed matrix membranes have been investigated, and the zeolites (inorganic)  have  presented  advantages  in  terms  of  physical  and  chemical  stability,  in  addition  to  greater selectivity. However, commercially available membranes for the separation of CO2  and CH4  are mostly polymeric, due to their higher stage of development.
texts.text: This review discusses recent advances in the development of materials and membranes with the potential for use in hostile environments and their efficiency in separating CO2  and CH4. Depending on the gas treatment operating condition, the membranes are exposed to harsh conditions (pressures above 40 bar), which is a challenge to ensure performance. Polymeric, inorganic, and mixed matrix membranes have been investigated, and the zeolites (inorganic)  have  presented  advantages  in  terms  of  physical  and  chemical  stability,  in  addition  to  greater selectivity. However, commercially available membranes for the separation of CO2  and CH4  are mostly polymeric, due to their higher stage of development.

texts.orig: (onshore)  or  underwater  (offshore)  (Reis  et  al.,  2017;  Araújo  et  al., 2017). Several processes have been used to remove the acid gases from the natural gas in a process called gas sweetening. Examples include adsorption, chemical absorption, cryogenic distillation, and membrane permeation, which are chosen according to the operating conditions and location of each reservoir. Membrane-based gas sweetening is of great importance to offshore platforms because the unit has a smaller footprint, high modularity, lower cost, and easier operation compared to other processes (Barbosa et al., 2019; Lee and Kim, 2020; Dalane et al., 2017, 2019).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Most of the reservoirs are underwater, and some can reach depths of up to 2000 m of water and localized far from the coast. The onshore and offshore explorations are mature processes for natural gas production (Tay  et  al.,  2019).  Recently,  the  major  global  oil  companies  have invested in research for installing subsea platforms (Ruud et al., 2015) and it is expected that these facilities can be completely subsea shortly (Gyllenhammar et al., 2015). This type of installation will deal with more severe operating conditions on the ocean floor than on platforms or offshore plants, motivating the use of membrane processes for gas treatment due to their intrinsic advantages (Offshore Energy Today and Ak, 2019). The expectation is that the subsea platforms could operate in reservoirs furthest from the coast, colder regions, deep waters, and in mature  wells,  increasing  oil  and  gas  recovery  (Dalane  et  al.,  2017; Ø kland et al., 2013).

The conditions are different from that of the on/offshore context in which the natural gas stream passes through several stages before going to the membrane skid where the separation occurs without causing further damage and at lower pressures due to the natural pressure drop. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Some physical properties of seawater, such as pressure and temperature, can be directly measured. However, direct measurements can be complicated to carry out, especially in the field. In several cases, it is more  convenient  to  estimate  the  desired  property  from  laboratory measurements. At a depth of 1000 m, pressure is 100 bar (Pawlowicz, 2013), and the seawater temperature is typically in the range of 2 -4 ◦ C, even though reservoirs can reach over 150 ◦ C internally (Xiao et al., 2020). Current researches have evaluated the NG purification at mild conditions, such as the study conducted by Dalane et al. (2019), which simulated subsea operating conditions at 30 ◦ C.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The use of membranes for purifying natural gas has been reported since 1994, and substantial advances in the development of membranes resistant to the hostile conditions of the natural gas stream are observed. However,  the  membranes  under  investigation,  mainly  polymeric, experienced plasticization and reduced selectivity when exposed to high pressures, which is a limiting factor to upgrade from the laboratory to the industrial scale (Tanco et al., 2018a; Zhang et al., 2017).
texts.text: The use of membranes for purifying natural gas has been reported since 1994, and substantial advances in the development of membranes resistant to the hostile conditions of the natural gas stream are observed. However,  the  membranes  under  investigation,  mainly  polymeric, experienced plasticization and reduced selectivity when exposed to high pressures, which is a limiting factor to upgrade from the laboratory to the industrial scale (Tanco et al., 2018a; Zhang et al., 2017).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Most commercial membranes are made from polymers due to the high degree of technological development over the years and the easier processability of these materials. These features contribute to lowering costs  of  material  processing  and,  consequently, the product obtained when  compared  to  inorganic  materials  such  as  carbon  or  ceramics (Iulianelli  and  Drioli,  2020a;  Robeson,  2008).  However,  commercial polymer membranes have presented low selectivity in the separation of CO2/CH4, requiring two separation steps to ensure high product purity. Besides,  they  present  low  mechanical  resistance  in  hostile  operating conditions and plasticize at high CO2 concentrations (Lasseuguette et al., 2019; Tanco et al., 2018b).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: An alternative frequently investigated is the combination of polymeric and inorganic materials by preparing the so-called Mixed Matrix Membranes (MMMs), in an attempt to guarantee the advantages of each material (Kang et al., 2016; Qian et al., 2021). These membranes are formed by incorporating an inorganic filler into a continuous polymeric matrix. The processability of the material is improved due to the presence of the polymer, while the transport of gases across the membrane is increased due to the inorganic fillers of intrinsic nanopores (He et al., 2018; George et al., 2016; Santaniello and Golemme, 2018).

These membranes are formed by incorporating an inorganic filler into a continuous polymeric matrix. The processability of the material is improved due to the presence of the polymer, while the transport of gases across the membrane is increased due to the inorganic fillers of intrinsic nanopores (He et al., 2018; George et al., 2016; Santaniello and Golemme, 2018).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Yet, even after more than 20 years of development, to the best of our knowledge,  there  are  no  MMMs  in  industrial  operation.  Although selectivity of MMMs like the amorphous ZIF-8/polyimide outperforms the commercial membranes, limitations during membrane production still  hinder the scale-up. The main problems are the lack of homogeneous dispersion, and the low adherence between the filler phase and the  polymer  continuous  phase,  which  usually  results  in  a  selectivity decrease (Li et al., 2013; Zagho et al., 2021; Bastani et al., 2013; Cheng et al., 2018).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This review brings the most recent studies regarding the development  of  membranes  exclusively  designed  for  natural  gas  treatment under  harsh  conditions.  The  parameter  used  as  the  cut-off  point  for selecting the studies was the minimum feed pressure of 40 bar, although this can still be considered low if we consider the conditions observed at the subsea level. As reported by He et al. (2020) and Chu and He (2018), currently available membranes have reduced efficiency when operating at  pressures higher than 40 bar. This is still the main obstacle to be overcome for improving membrane efficiency under real conditions of natural gas purification (Bachman and Long, 2016). The review covers the  main  materials  being  used  to  prepare  membranes  used  in  such conditions, their limitations, types of module, and points out that most used  and  promising  for  gas  separation  with  emphasis  on  CO2/CH4 separation.

texts.orig: Given the different types of membranes that can separate the CO2/ CH4 gas pair, different separation mechanisms are expected. Most of the polymeric membranes have a dense selective layer, in which gas transport  is  governed  by  the  solution-diffusion  mechanism  (Wang  et  al., 2016; Hayek et al., 2019). Inorganic membranes have a porous structure. Consequently, the transport is dependent on the pore size (molecular sieving for pore size < 0.6 nm, surface diffusion for pore size < 2 nm, and Knudsen diffusion when the pore size is in the range 2 -50 nm) (Gitis and Rothenberg, 2016). In the MMMs, the transport mechanism combines both the common solution-diffusion of the dense polymer and the mechanisms provided by each type of filler (inorganic material). For this  type  of  membrane,  the  interface  between  the  materials  also  influences the transport of the gas, and it should be thus carefully investigated (Wang et al., 2015, 2017).

texts.orig: Although membranes are widely used in natural gas purification, this process is not as selective as the absorption with amines, for example, and part of the methane is lost (Lasseuguette et al., 2019). It is possible to increase the methane recovery by installing a second separation stage, as shown in Fig. 1. Two stages of membranes are combined with other separation processes resulting in a hybrid and more efficient system for removing CO2  from natural gas. The reinjection in the reservoir is the main destination of CO2 to maintain pressure and guarantee production over the years of exploration (Barbosa et al., 2019; Iulianelli and Drioli, 2020a).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3. Membranes designed for high-pressure separation of CO2/CH4
texts.text: 3. Membranes designed for high-pressure separation of CO2/CH4

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Several  studies  regarding  the  development  of  membranes  for  the separation of the CO2/CH4 pair are carried out at ambient temperature (~25 ◦ C), low feed pressure ( < 40 bar), and pure gas permeation. This fact  is  reasonable  since  more  severe  conditions  require  special  and expensive equipment, and the natural gas on exploration platforms is usually treated at pressures below 60 bar. Indeed, these researches are important to find new materials, design membrane modules, and understand the transport mechanisms of the gases through the membranes. It is noteworthy, though, that not all articles that appear throughout the discussion were selected to the figures and tables presented in this review because we restricted to the studies investigating CO2/CH4  separation  at  the  minimum pressure of 40 bar. Given that the polymeric commercial membranes plasticize and lose selectivity at high CO2 concentrations, the development of new membrane materials may increase the  application  of  membrane  processes  in  natural  gas  processing (Ahmad et al., 2015; Lei et al., 2020; Haider et al., 2018; Baker and Lokhandwala, 2008).

texts.orig: In search of increasingly efficient and resistant membranes, research has advanced not only in the modification of the polymeric structure through intrinsic porosity (PIM) (Wang et al., 2019), crosslinking, or heat  treatment,  but  also  in  the  insertion  of  additives  with  different functionalities in the polymeric matrix (Wu and Jiang, 2021; Luo et al., 2018; Nunhen et al., 2018; Woli ´ nska-Grabczyk et al., 2017). Fig. 2 illustrates  the  permeability  and  selectivity  data  for  membranes  of different materials operating above 40 bar, and the lines corresponding to the upper limit proposed by Robeson in 2008 (Robeson, 2008) and mixed gas 2018 CO2/CH4 upper bound proposed by Wang et al. (2019). It  is  clear  that  zeolite  and  carbon  membranes,  which  are  inorganic materials, exceed the bound line.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.1. Polymeric membranes
texts.text: 3.1. Polymeric membranes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The current industrial natural gas plants based on membranes use almost exclusively polymeric membranes, like polyimide (PI), cellulose acetate  (CA),  and  polyamide  (PA).  Polymers  are  easier  to  shape  in different types of membranes/modules and therefore are more economical than other materials. Nevertheless, they have limitations when  operating  in  harsh  conditions.  Plasticization,  physical  aging, compaction at high pressure, and the permeability/selectivity trade-off are  some  challenges  to  be  overcome  (Iulianelli  and  Drioli,  2020a; Robeson, 2008; George et al., 2016; Dai et al., 2016; Favvas et al., 2017). A  selection  of  the  main  polymers  investigated  for  the  separation  of CO2/CH4 at high pressures is presented in Table 1.

Nevertheless, they have limitations when  operating  in  harsh  conditions.  Plasticization,  physical  aging, compaction at high pressure, and the permeability/selectivity trade-off are  some  challenges  to  be  overcome  (Iulianelli  and  Drioli,  2020a; Robeson, 2008; George et al., 2016; Dai et al., 2016; Favvas et al., 2017). A  selection  of  the  main  polymers  investigated  for  the  separation  of CO2/CH4 at high pressures is presented in Table 1.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The studies were all carried out on a laboratory scale, comparing pure gas and mixed gas permeation. The gas transport through dense polymer membranes is a complex process, dependent on the characteristics  of  the  material,  of  the  feed  gas,  temperature,  and  pressure, which affect the sorption and the diffusion coefficients (solution-diffusion mechanism) (Ismail et al., 2015). According to the solution-diffusion mechanism, the permeation occurs in three steps: the dissolution of the gas in the polymer at the high pressure, followed by the diffusion of the gas through the polymeric film resulting from the concentration gradient, and finally the desorption downstream of this gas  (Hayek  et  al.,  2019;  Adewole  et  al.,  2015).  The  permeability  of low-sorbing gases in glassy polymers usually exhibits a small change in permeability with increasing pressure, while it increases with pressure for high-sorbing gases such as carbon dioxide (Yampolskii et al., 2006). The high condensability of the CO2 with its high concentration at high pressures  leads  to  the  plasticization  effect  (an  increase  of  the  chain mobility  so  that  diffusion  and  permeation  coefficients  increase  with increasing pressure) (Yampolskii et al., 2006; Yampolskii and Freeman, 2010).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Glassy polymers tend to favor CO2/CH4 separation due to the smaller size  and  high  condensability  of  CO2 (Bachman  and  Long,  2016; AlQahtani and Mezghani, 2018; Liu et al., 2020b). In the study conducted by Liu et al. (2020b), 6FDA-based polyimides presented good efficiency in the binary separation of H2S/CH4 and CO2/CH4 pairs. Both sorption  and  diffusion  coefficients  favored  the  CO2/CH4 separation, while the sorption was dominant for the H2S/CH4 pair.

texts.orig: The limitation of polymeric membranes in subsea installations is the tendency  towards  plasticization  and  physical  aging,  a  common  phenomenon in membranes exposed to high pressures of soluble components such as CO2, in addition to the presence of heavy hydrocarbons. As the simplification of the subsea units is prioritized, and pre-treatment steps  may  be  removed,  it  is  required  membranes  resistant  to  almost crude NG feed (Okazaki et al., 2019; Dalane et al., 2017; Favvas et al., 2017; Minelli et al., 2018).

texts.orig: Glassy polymer membranes have shown better potential for resistance in the CO2/CH4 separation in more aggressive conditions, but still have limitations regarding the plasticization and reduced CO2  permeability in mixtures with a high number of components (Cheng et al., 2018; Lei et al., 2020; Adewole et al., 2015). Fluorinated polyimides, for example, exhibit great characteristics to be used at high pressures since they keep their mechanical properties even in severe operating conditions. This class of polymers shows rigidity, high thermal and chemical stability  (Liu  et  al.,  2020a;  Iulianelli  and  Drioli,  2020b;  Hamid  and Jeong, 2018; Yi et al., 2019).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The most common process for preparing polymeric membranes is phase inversion, where the polymer is dissolved in a solvent, then spread on a flat surface or Petri dish. The addition of another component (nonsolvent or temperature change) is responsible for destabilizing the solution and forming the membrane (Ismail et al., 2015; Drioli and Giorno, 2010). In the case of 6FDA-based polyimides, such as those in Table 1, the  preparation  of  the  membranes  takes  place  through  the  polycondensation reaction, which consists of the precursor formation step, followed  by  the  imidation  step  (Hayek  et  al.,  2019,  2020).  These membranes can be dense isotropic or anisotropic. The latter consists of a very thin selective layer that covers a porous support and it is of greater industrial interest due to the higher permeate flux.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Some  strategies  are  used  to  improve  the  resistance  of  polymer membranes  when  exposed  to  more  aggressive  feed  conditions.  The thermal  rearrangement  of  aromatic  polyimides  or  polyamides  (Tena et al.,  2016) at high temperatures (~450 ◦ C) creates inter-connected free-volume elements that have characteristic diameters in the range of  4 -9  Å,  allowing  the  separation  of  molecules  of  different  sizes. AlQahtani and Mezghani (2018) showed that even operating at a pressure of 55 bar, the thermally rearranged polypyrrole membranes showed high resistance to plasticization and efficiency in the removal of CO2 in the ternary mixture.

texts.orig: Another option for increasing polymer resistance is crosslinking and grafting. For instance, the cross-linked copolymers 4,4 ' -(hexafluoroisopropylidene)  diphthalic  anhydride  (6FDA-based)  are  very common and show good efficiency in the separation of carbon dioxide and methane due to the presence of bulky hexafluoroisopropylidene C (CF3)2 that  can  improve  the  solubility  by  modifying  the  membrane morphology  (Hayek  et  al.,  2019;  Achoundong  et  al.,  2013;  Sorribas et al., 2020).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.2. Zeolite membranes
texts.text: 3.2. Zeolite membranes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Zeolite is a class of crystalline aluminosilicates composed of a 3D pore structure with a uniform pore size between 2 and 20 Å. Membranes made of zeolites have the potential to yield high selectivities due to the tight pore size of the zeolites that form the selective layer. Other advantages of zeolite membranes are their selective and catalytic adsorption  capacity  (Chang  et  al.,  2018;  Ismail  et  al.,  2015;  Gascon  et  al., 2012). Of the wide variety of synthetic zeolites available, two are the most  used  for  gas  separation  at  high  pressures:  the  DDR  (deca-dodecacil3R)  and  the  SAPO-34  (silico-alumino-phosphate).  Scanning electron microscopy images of membranes containing a thin selective layer of SAPO-34 zeolites tested for CO2/CH4 separation at high pressure are shown in Fig. 3. It is possible to note a relatively non-uniform surface, characteristic of the three-dimensional framework of the zeolites. In addition, the selective layer with around 5 -10 μ m thickness is usual for this kind of membrane (Li et al., 2008; Rehman et al., 2019; Tomita et al., 2004).

In addition, the selective layer with around 5 -10 μ m thickness is usual for this kind of membrane (Li et al., 2008; Rehman et al., 2019; Tomita et al., 2004).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The DDR type is one of the zeolites with eight-membered oxygen rings  (8  MR)  that  unite  the  silica  polyhedron.  Due  to  this  structural characteristic, the porous materials of this group have the potential to recover small molecules of CO2 from natural gas and separate them from other light gases (Mubashir et al., 2014; Karamouz et al., 2018). There are three types of DDR: DD3R, Sigma-1, and ZSM-58 (Hayakawa and Himeno, 2020).
texts.text: The DDR type is one of the zeolites with eight-membered oxygen rings  (8  MR)  that  unite  the  silica  polyhedron.  Due  to  this  structural characteristic, the porous materials of this group have the potential to recover small molecules of CO2 from natural gas and separate them from other light gases (Mubashir et al., 2014; Karamouz et al., 2018). There are three types of DDR: DD3R, Sigma-1, and ZSM-58 (Hayakawa and Himeno, 2020).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The most hydrophobic is the DD3R because it contains the highest amount of silica. Due to its configuration, it has small pores with an opening  of  0.36 -0.44  nm,  indicated  for  the  separation  of  CO2/CH4, which  have  molecular  sizes  of  0.33  and  0.38  nm,  respectively.  It  is possible to find zeolite membranes with selectivity greater than 500 for this pair of gases (Ismail et al., 2015; Hayakawa and Himeno, 2020; Zito et al., 2020).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Despite the potential of the zeolite membranes, the only commercial membrane made of DDR for purification of natural gas is supplied by NGK, as described in detail in Section 5. It is reported that the carbon dioxide  permeability  of  the  NGK  membrane  is  ~2000  Barrer.  This membrane presents high selectivity ( α CO2/CH4 > 140) when compared to polymeric counterparts, thus indicating a low methane loss (a common problem in conventional membranes). Another advantage of using DDR membranes is the high-pressure resistance and the absence of the plasticization effect (Okazaki et al., 2019; Kosinov et al., 2016; Hasegawa et al., 2017).

texts.orig: The high selectivity of SAPO-34 zeolite in the separation of CO2/CH4 gases  is  the  result  of  its  great  interaction  with  polar  compounds,  in addition  to  its  small  pore  size  (0.38  nm).  Another  advantage  is  the robustness and stability in more severe environments, such as that in natural gas sweetening. This zeolite presents strong adsorption of CO2, dependent on temperature and pressure, while other gases such as N2, CH4 are more strongly guided by diffusion (Makertihartha et al., 2020; Xu  et  al.,  2021).  Its  intrinsic  polarity  due  to  the  aluminum  in  the structure confers high hydrophilicity to the zeolite. Low hydrophilicity and hydrothermal stability may be a limiting factor in environments with the presence of water vapor when compared with DDR (pure silica zeolite) (Hayakawa and Himeno, 2020; Zito et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2021).

SAPO-34 membranes are sensitive to impurities present in NG and present low separation at high temperatures, which are obstacles to be overcome to guarantee their industrial application (Makertihartha et al., 2020; Kosinov et al., 2016; Chen et al., 2017).

texts.orig: The gas transport mechanisms observed in zeolite membranes are adsorption,  diffusion,  and  molecular  sieving.  In  adsorption,  the  predominant process at  low  temperatures,  separation  occurs  due  to  the greater affinity that one of the feed components has with the membrane. Diffusion is dominant at higher temperatures and is also influenced by the size of the molecule, diameter, and pore geometry. The main aspect observed in molecular sieving is the exclusion by size taking into account the kinetic diameter of each gas molecule. Adsorption, in turn, is the result of interactions that depend on the polarity (Si/Al ratio) of the zeolite and occur, for example, in molecules with quadrupole moment, such as CO2 (Makertihartha et al., 2020; Woli ´ nska-Grabczyk et al., 2017; Jee et al., 2009; Loloei et al., 2015).

texts.orig: 3.3. Carbon membranes
texts.text: 3.3. Carbon membranes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Among the material options developed in the last decades, the carbon  molecular  sieves  (CMS)  present  the  best  relationship  between effectiveness  and  stability  (Yoshimune  and  Haraya,  2013).  These membranes are attractive to the industry because of their high selectivity  in  the  separation  of  gases  even  under  high  pressure  and  high temperature (Chu and He, 2018; Haider et al., 2018; Karousos et al., 2020a).  The  great  advantage  of  carbon-based  materials  is  the  high porosity  (formed  by  ultramicropores  and  micropores,  with  pore  size similar to the size of the molecules), which yields high selectivity and high  permeability  (Hamm  et  al.,  2017;  Rungta  et  al.,  2017).  Some membranes found in the literature that meet these more severe conditions are shown in Table 3.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Carbon  membranes are  obtained  from  the  pyrolysis  of  polymeric precursors  and  have  different  properties  depending  on  the  source polymer  and  pyrolysis  conditions.  The  selection  of  an  adequate  precursor polymer is important because it influences the final characteristics of the CMS (Lei et al., 2020; Vu et al., 2002b). Despite the removal of most of the original heteroatoms from the polymer during pyrolysis, yielding an almost complete carbon structure, characteristics such as pendant groups, glass transition temperature, and free volume influence the CMS properties (Hamm et al., 2017; Rungta et al., 2017).

texts.orig: Studies have been carried out to guarantee tailored microstructure that  improves  the  stability  and  efficiency  of  carbon  membranes  in separating gases at high pressures. Haider et al. (2018) synthesized a CMS with modified geometry from cellulose acetate pyrolyzed up to 550 ◦ C. They tested the membranes at 70 bar for CO2/CH4  separation and observed that the increase in pressure did not interfere with the properties of the membrane, while the increase in temperature to 120 ◦ C caused a reduction of approximately 50% in CO2 permeability.

texts.orig: Transport in carbon membranes is guided by adsorption and activated transport through selective pores (Haider et al., 2018). Under high pressures, the sorption of CO2 is stronger, due to the structure of CMS, micropores,  and  ultramicropores,  the  latter  being  responsible  for providing entropically enabled diffusion selectivities (by restricting the degree of freedom of molecules), significantly improving transport when compared  to  polymeric  membranes  (Zhang  et  al.,  2017;  Vu  et  al., 2002b).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  mixed  matrix  membranes  (MMMs)  typically  present  better transport properties than membranes prepared from pure polymer or filler precursors. Fillers are divided into porous and nonporous materials, as seen in Fig. 5. In the first class, zeolite, carbon molecular sieves, and metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are capable of directly changing the permeability or selectivity via their porous network (He et al., 2018; Li et al., 2013).

Fillers are divided into porous and nonporous materials, as seen in Fig. 5. In the first class, zeolite, carbon molecular sieves, and metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are capable of directly changing the permeability or selectivity via their porous network (He et al., 2018; Li et al., 2013).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Nonporous fillers such as silica nanoparticles (Shafie et al., 2019), titanium (Mustafa et al., 2017), and graphene oxide (Feijani et al., 2018) can modify the diffusion path via changing the amount of free volume of the organic phase and improving the selective affinity for desired species,  thus  influencing  separation  mechanisms  (Tanco  et  al.,  2018a; Maleh and Raisi, 2019; Vandezande et al., 2015).

Another important parameter to be observed is the compatibility  between filler  and polymer, to ensure that there is neither the formation of voids nor the rigidification and/or pore blockage of the fillers in contact with the polymer (Zagho et al., 2021; Bastani et al., 2013; Chang and Chang, 2018).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The choice of polymer is important to ensure the ideal properties of MMMs  for  the  desired  separation.  Rubbery  polymers  facilitate  the achievement of a MMM free of defects due to the greater chain mobility, which improves the adhesion of fillers between the chains. Their limitation is the low mechanical strength and low selectivity for industrial applications (Bastani et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2016, 2017; Hamid and Jeong, 2018; Jusoh et al., 2016). Glassy polymers present rigid chains that  provide  greater  free  volume,  better  mechanical  resistance,  and greater selectivity but are limited by the higher incidence of plasticization and physical aging (Iulianelli and Drioli, 2020a; Cheng et al., 2018; Chaidou et al., 2012; Smith et al., 2020).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In addition to the different materials that influence the efficiency of the  gas  separation  process,  the  membrane  geometry  and  module configuration are very relevant aspects to consider (George et al., 2016). In the case of the carbon dioxide separation from natural gas, the choice of  geometry  and  module  is  even  more  meaningful  due  to  the  harsh conditions the units are submitted. Membrane modules can be classified according to the membrane geometry (Fig. 6) as flat or tubular (cylindrical).  Both  are  commonly  used  in  gas  purification  processes,  as detailed in the following sections.

texts.orig: Alternatively, there are three types of tubular membranes usually classified  according  to  the  tube  (decreasing)  diameter:  tubular  ( > 10 mm), capillary (0.5 -10 mm), and hollow fibers ( < 0.5 mm). The tubular modules can operate with the feed stream containing large particles, a limiting  factor  for  the  capillary  and  hollow  fibers  due  to  the  risk  of blockage if the selective layer is located at the lumen side. The tubular modules  operate  in  turbulent  regimes  with  Reynolds  above  10,000, while the hollow fiber modules can operate only with Reynolds in the range of 500 -3,000, which is considered a laminar regime (Ismail et al., 2015; Cui et al., 2010).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Polymeric membranes are prevalent in the industry even in the most aggressive  feed  conditions.  Table  6  presents  commercial  membranes used  to  sweeten  natural  gas.  Evonik  recently  (2018)  launched  the Sepuran-NG hollow fiber module, which is resistant to high temperatures, high pressures, and high CO2  concentration. The Separex/UOP membrane has been on the market for a long time and more than 130 units  are  installed  around  the  world.  This  membrane  removes  both acidic  compounds  and  water  from  natural  gas.  The  Cynara  Semple/ Schlumberger membrane can also operate under severe conditions and has the advantage of more compact size and less weight when compared to Separex, due to its high surface area (Alcheikhhamdon and Hoorfar, 2016; Atkinson, 2018).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 6. Conclusions and future outlook
texts.text: 6. Conclusions and future outlook

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Recent interest in the natural gas exploration from deepwater wells has driven researchers to find alternatives for the in-situ gas treatment, i. e., subsea processing units. Although the membrane-based separation process is already used in offshore units for natural gas treatment, the operating conditions are considerably milder than those in subsea units since the raw natural gas is submitted to several pre-treatment steps before  membrane  separation.  The  use  of  membranes  in  subsea  installations represents a great challenge because ideally, the natural gas should  not  pass  through  a  pre-treatment  step,  going  directly  to  the membrane skid to guarantee a simpler installation with fewer steps.

Although the membrane-based separation process is already used in offshore units for natural gas treatment, the operating conditions are considerably milder than those in subsea units since the raw natural gas is submitted to several pre-treatment steps before  membrane  separation.  The  use  of  membranes  in  subsea  installations represents a great challenge because ideally, the natural gas should  not  pass  through  a  pre-treatment  step,  going  directly  to  the membrane skid to guarantee a simpler installation with fewer steps.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The membrane for subsea installations must operate in conditions above 80 bar, while the modules and membrane skid should not be too heavy, given that it has to be installed in ultra-deep waters (above 2000 m depth). The expectation that subsea platforms will soon become a reality  is  very  high.  In  2019,  a  Joint  Industry  Project  on  subsea  gas separation was launched, bringing together the largest companies in the world in this segment of the gas industry. Their focus is the development of  membranes  that  meet  operational  conditions  as  there  are  none currently in operation. For more information about the project, access (https://www.akersolutions.com).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The literature is extensive in addressing the natural gas treatment using membrane processes. Nevertheless, there is a large gap regarding membranes capable of acting in more hostile feed conditions. One of the reasons for the lack of studies carried out in conditions closer to the real ambient may be the high investment required to design and build the equipment that supports such conditions, in addition to safety issues. Moreover, the scaling from lab to pilot scale would require the behavior of these materials when exposed to this scenario. Besides, there is a lack of  studies  with  multicomponent  feed  compositions  similar  to  those found in raw natural gas. Most of the papers are focused only on testing pure CO2 and CH4 feed streams and calculating a theoretical selectivity, and binary CO2/CH4  mixtures. The impurities present in NG, even in small concentrations, can influence the efficiency of the membranes and compromise their selectivity and integrity.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This critical review provides insights into developing nextgeneration membranes with superior performance for natural gas purification, more specifically the carbon dioxide removal. Glassy polymers are considered suitable as a matrix or a precursor for gas separation membranes, especially at high pressures. Despite having great potential in CO2/CH4, zeolites such as DDR, SAPO-34, and 13X, and the different forms of carbon membranes, still have a large development path to be competitive  to  the  commercial  polymer  membranes.  Moreover,  the competitive sorption in the presence of water vapor, the greater weight of the modules, and their fragility, which increase the difficulty in the system installation, handling, and maintenance have to be considered.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The hollow fiber and honeycomb membranes are identified as the most promising geometries due to their great packing density and suitability for both offshore and subsea applications. It is important that robust membranes, regardless of material, are stable over a wide range of pressure and temperature, as natural gas feed can change composition over the years (increase of CO2 concentration that has been reinjected).
